Superior Court 
720 Ninth Street 
Sacramento, CA

Dear Judge Babich: 
February 22, 1982 
Stoftments of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice from the 
Fair Political Practices Commission. 
The facts, as you pre-
sented them to me, are that you attended a Judici.ll Conf~H·en~f.) 
on Land Use Planning and Regulation sponsored by three California 
law schools. 
Your wife accompanied you to this conference 
which was held in the Napa Valley. 
All of the expenses (meals, 
lodgings and mileage) for both of you were completely paid 
for and amounted to $400. 
You received no money in excess of 
your expenses for attending the conference. 
A brochure you 
sent me states that the conference was made possible through 
grants to the University of California from five private 
entities, the majority of which are involved with the building 
industry. 
Your question is whether the $400 paid for you and your 
wife's expenses must be reported on your Statement of Economic 
Interests as a gift. 
Since the ~ctual invitation to the 
conference came from educational organizations and because 
the brochure states that the grants used for the conference 
were first given to the University of California, the money 
you received for your portion of the expenses constitutes 
"reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from 
a bona fide educational [or] academic •.. organization." 
As 
such, it is exempt from the Act's definition of "income" and 
you are not required to report it. 
(See Government Code 
Section 82030{b)(2).) 
With respect to the reimbursements made on behalf of 
your spouse, it is the Commission's policy that unless the 
donor, in making such a payment, intended to influence the 
official or make an indirect gift to the official, then the 


Honorable Joseph Babich 
February 22, 1982 
Page Two 
gift is the separate property ofl1he spouse and need not be 
reported by the public official.-
Additionally, her share 
would also be exempt from the reporting requirements as reimbursement 
for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide 
educational organization. 
If you have any additional questions, please feel free 
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

SACRAMENTO. CALIFORNIA 
January 29, 1982 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K street Mall 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Attention: 
Ms. Alice Hughes 
Dear 1'ls. Hughe s : 
I am requesting informal advice in regard to whether 
or not I should report as a gift the expenses totaling $400 
for my wife and me in attending the JUdicial Conference on 
Land Use Planning and Regulation, sponsored by the organiza-
tions listed in the enclosed document. 
This was a seminar on land use and planning where both 
the conservationists (no-growth) and expansionists (growth) 
gave their views in this area of the law. 
I would appreciate your informal opinion in this 
regard. 
Sincerely, 
JOSEPH G. BABICH 
Judge of the Superior Court

sections.question: Your question is whether the $400 paid for you and your wife's expenses must be reported on your Statement of Economic Interests as a gift.
sections.conclusion: Since the actual invitation to the conference came from educational organizations and because the brochure states that the grants used for the conference were first given to the University of California, the money you received for your portion of the expenses constitutes 'reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide educational [or] academic ... organization.' As such, it is exempt from the Act's definition of 'income' and you are not required to report it. (See Government Code Section 82030{b}(2).) With respect to the reimbursements made on behalf of your spouse, it is the Commission's policy that unless the donor, in making such a payment, intended to influence the official or make an indirect gift to the official, then the gift is the separate property of the spouse and need not be reported by the public official. Additionally, her share would also be exempt from the reporting requirements as reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide educational organization.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a judge must report on a Statement of Economic Interest the $400 in conference expenses (meals, lodging, mileage) for himself and his wife paid by a judicial conference sponsored by California law schools and funded by private building industry grants.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The expenses need not be reported because they constitute reimbursement from a bona fide educational organization under Government Code Section 82030(b)(2), which is exempt from the definition of reportable income. The spouse's expenses are similarly exempt both as a separate property gift (absent intent to influence) and as reimbursement from the educational organization.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter structure with incoming request letter followed by FPPC response letter. Question and conclusion sections explicitly identified. Conference brochure included as supporting document. Minor OCR artifacts present but content clearly legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether the $400 paid for you and your wife's expenses must be reported on your Statement of Economic Interests as a gift.

CONCLUSION: Since the actual invitation to the conference came from educational organizations and because the brochure states that the grants used for the conference were first given to the University of California, the money you received for your portion of the expenses constitutes 'reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide educational [or] academic ... organization.' As such, it is exempt from the Act's definition of 'income' and you are not required to report it. (See Government Code Section 82030{b}(2).) With respect to the reimbursements made on behalf of your spouse, it is the Commission's policy that unless the donor, in making such a payment, intended to influence the official or make an indirect gift to the official, then the gift is the separate property of the spouse and need not be reported by the public official. Additionally, her share would also be exempt from the reporting requirements as reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide educational organization.

embedding.summary: A Sacramento Superior Court judge sought advice on whether to report $400 in conference expenses (for himself and his wife) paid by a judicial conference sponsored by law schools and funded by private grants. The FPPC advised that the expenses need not be reported as they constitute exempt reimbursement from a bona fide educational organization under Government Code Section 82030(b)(2).
